title: The Myth of the ‘Best’ Options Strategy (Here’s What Actually Works)
text: What's up outliers? Today we are going to talk about the absolute best option strategy. I'm going to walk you through seven traits that we need to look for to identify what we would consider to be the best and then I'm going to give you some ideas to think about with respect to actual strategies that might fit the bill for us. Now, as you know, options are very complex. However, they offer us exposure to a lot of really interesting ways to make money. So, when we're looking at the best option strategy, one of the most important elements of this is something that can trade different market conditions because the market goes through different regimes. If we pop over to the platform and we take a look at a fiveyear weekly chart in S&P, you can see that most of the time S&P is upwards trending. So bullish direction. However, you can also see that there are periods like 2022, which is this entire time frame in here where it actually declined. So being able to trade multiple market conditions is going to be really really important for the best option strategy. We need something that we can consistently make money off of. That reliability is one of the most important elements of a strategy because if it's something that regularly produces the results that we expect and they're positively expectant, meaning they're profitable, then it's

title: The Myth of the ‘Best’ Options Strategy (Here’s What Actually Works)
text: money off of. That reliability is one of the most important elements of a strategy because if it's something that regularly produces the results that we expect and they're positively expectant, meaning they're profitable, then it's something we can continue doing in the future, which is really important must have. We want to see things that have larger winning trades relative to the losing positions. Why is this important? Well, it's for convexity. One of the issues with really high win rate strategies, pure high win rate strategies, is although they win a lot, they tend to win not that much money. So, here's a great example of this. If we go into S&P and we go into short-term options. So, let's use these 3-day options for an example. I can come out here and we can go super far out of the money and I can sell these two delta puts and I can get like $90 for these two delta puts. So, this means that the overwhelming probability is that these are going to expire out of the money. However, this is what we refer to as selling the tails because every once in a while you will get caught. Meaning there will be a big move and when you experience that one but rare big move, it can wipe out multiples of winners, which is a big problem. Now, this leads to our next

title: The Myth of the ‘Best’ Options Strategy (Here’s What Actually Works)
text: get caught. Meaning there will be a big move and when you experience that one but rare big move, it can wipe out multiples of winners, which is a big problem. Now, this leads to our next point where we would prefer something that has undefined profit potential. And that's simply because if we're correct in terms of our direction, we don't want to cap our potential losses to that to our potential gains. To that same vein, we do want to cap our losses because again going back to this scenario if we choose to sell this 5765 put that expires in 3 days. Let's analyze this trade and let's just say that there happens to be a massive move against us. Let's say that the market drops 15% come Monday because over the weekend there's some really terrible news that comes out. or even if it drops 10%. Or even 5%. What you'll notice is that even on a 5% decline, while we're in here trying to make 85 or 90 bucks, we're going to lose 7,624. So the probability of S&P dropping this 5% over the weekend, opening Monday, and then trending down is low, but you can literally do the math to see how many trades you would wipe out with this one. $7,600 loser, which is like 89. So, the issue with undefined losses is that they can rack up very, very quickly unless

title: The Myth of the ‘Best’ Options Strategy (Here’s What Actually Works)
text: math to see how many trades you would wipe out with this one. $7,600 loser, which is like 89. So, the issue with undefined losses is that they can rack up very, very quickly unless there's a control in place for them. We also would want something that benefits from theta decay, which is the standard holding penalty for long options. And we would prefer something that benefits from gamma because again that provides convexity to our return the ability for the actual return profile to accelerate. So now that we know the traits of the best option strategy, what fits the bill? And this will be of absolutely no surprise to you. Nothing. Nothing fits this bill. There is no option strategy that does all of this. It doesn't exist. And this is the problem when we try to frame things like what is the best option strategy is we're trying to be lazy. We're trying to say what is the best thing I can do that always works optimally. Guess what? That doesn't exist. Now, there's a benefit from all of this. You can absolutely build relative best option strategies and we use something like the outlier strategy process to help us do this. I have a link that talks about this in detail in the notes below, but I want to review it quickly and show you

title: The Myth of the ‘Best’ Options Strategy (Here’s What Actually Works)
text: strategies and we use something like the outlier strategy process to help us do this. I have a link that talks about this in detail in the notes below, but I want to review it quickly and show you an exact example of this concept. So the outlier strategy process you will notice that there is absolutely a way to build a best strategy. The problem is the best strategy is going to be relative to specific profit mechanisms or market effects. You will not have something that provides the best across all forms because going back to this, we would prefer something that has large winners, relative losses, and ideally something with a high win rate. Those two don't typically go together. Same thing with wanting to benefit from theta decay, but then also benefiting from gamma. Those two typically go together. So the broader point here is if we take the time to isolate the actual profit mechanism, the market effect that we're trying to capitalize on, then we absolutely can find optimal structures that we turn into strategies for those very ideas. And I'm going to show you an example right now. So, if we go back into the platform and now I actually want to go into ondemand and I want to go back in time to show you an example. So based on this hunch, there are a couple different trades we can model. text: size position, the issue we run into now is that if we do get a continuation and if Nvidia continues to trend down, had to update the date for you, but if Nvidia dips down 10%, you lose 3,700 bucks. If it dips down 15%, you're now down 6,800 bucks. Comparatively to this, if Nvidia dips down 5%, you lose 270. If it dips down 10%, you lose 592. If it dips down 15%, you lose 865. So this immediately shows you the tradeoff of different options positions. So now I just want to fast forward time until as far forward as we can go just for the example. And I just want to compare these different positions for you because if I size this back down to just kind of compare them on a one lot basis. I want to show you a couple things. Now I need to update the date in here again. Let's go to the 19th. And so this long call at this point is showing us $952 profit. This short put is showing us $424 profit. So via the long call again, we picked a favorable condition, but for this specific profit mechanism, we make more money with this long call. Now, this still isn't optimized for exactly what happened. If we wanted to fully optimize for exactly what happened here, right, to overfit it entirely, we would

title: The Myth of the ‘Best’ Options Strategy (Here’s What Actually Works)
text: this specific profit mechanism, we make more money with this long call. Now, this still isn't optimized for exactly what happened. If we wanted to fully optimize for exactly what happened here, right, to overfit it entirely, we would have bought shortdated calls that were out of the money. But the general point I'm trying to show you is that if we're trying to trade something that quote unquote is the best and it means that we always do something, always buy an option, always sell an option, there are periods where both will underperform comparatively. And as a trader, you are so much better off outlining different profit mechanisms that you're going to attempt to trade. Again, in this case, the profit mechanism is a price reversion based on a breakout and overextension to the downside. So, in that kind of context, if we understand what this kind of price movement typically looks like, we would naturally back into saying, well, selling a put here might cap me short. And if I sold the put at the same size as the long call, I'm taking a ton of risk on a relative basis because I am in the process of playing something to the upside that had a pretty vicious down move and it can as always roll back over to the downside. So the best option strategy is going to be relative and built first around the profit mechanism

title: The Myth of the ‘Best’ Options Strategy (Here’s What Actually Works)
text: process of playing something to the upside that had a pretty vicious down move and it can as always roll back over to the downside. So the best option strategy is going to be relative and built first around the profit mechanism you're trying to trade and then the second something that fits in with your broader portfolio objectives. Be an outlier. See you later.
